The creators of the German law of “personality” interpreted the development of

the ancient Roman law of insult in the same way—as an evolution of the “spirit” of

120
    For a convenient collection of translated texts, see Bruce W. Frier, A Casebook on the Roman Law of
Delict (Atlanta: Scholars Press, 1989), 3-6, 177-200.
121
    Rudolf von Jhering, Rechtsschutz gegen injuriöse Rechtsverletzungen, in Gesammelte Aufsätze (Jena:
Fischer, 1886), 3: 234-235.

43
 Roman law, as Rudolf von Jhering, the most brilliant of German law professors, 122 called

it, from the material to the immaterial. The argument rans as follows. Honor had always

been at stake in the law of insult, even in the earliest periods. But at first, the Romans,

still obsessed with things, had thought that the law could only monetizable rights, mere

material rights. But as sensibilites about honor grew richer and deeper, these early legal

protections gradually ripened, until the law grew to cover all aspects of our honor,

protecting us also against verbal insults and other shows of disrespect. 123 The evolution

of the law of honor, like the evolution of the law of punishment, was thus an evolution in

the “spirit of the times”—one in which primitive protections for merely monetizable

interests had gradually matured into sophisticated protections for “non-economic”

interests. 124 That slow evolution, from the material to the immaterial, was moreover

continuing in the modern world: The modern world was now producing what Jhering

called, in a famous 1885 article, the law of “insulting tortious injuries.” In particular,

modern protections were now evolving beyond protections against immaterial verbal

insults, to include the protection of such immaterial goods as one’s name 125 and one’s

photographed image, 126 one’s control of one’s correspondence, 127 as well as access to

modern amenities such as the telegraph and the tram. 128

122
    Jhering, Der Geist des römischen Rechts auf den verschiedenen Stufen seiner Entwicklung, 5th ed.
(Leipzig: Breitkopf und Härtel, 1891-1906).

44
          Jhering was one of a number of German scholars to make this sort of argument,

some relying on ancient Roman law, some drawing on Germanic sources. 129 This

Hegelianized law of insult was one main strand in the new German law of personality.

The other was the law of Urheberrecht, creator’s rights. The rights of an artistic or

intellectual creator, in German law, were partly rights of copyright. But they also were
